title: Pollo alla Cacciatora
subtitle: Hunter's chicken — braised in tomatoes, white wine, and herbs
description: Cacciatora means 'hunter's style' in Italian, and every region of Italy has a version. This one follows the Central Italian tradition: bone-in chicken pieces browned in olive oil, then braised slowly in white wine, tinned tomatoes, olives, and rosemary until the chicken is fall-from-the-bone tender and the sauce has reduced to something thick and glossy. It improves substantially overnight and reheats beautifully, making it ideal for cooking ahead.
sourceNotes: Pollo alla cacciatora is documented in both Northern and Central Italian cooking traditions. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) contains an early version; the dish appears in Marcella Hazan's Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992) in a form close to this recipe.

recipeTools.notes: A wide casserole or deep-sided sauté pan, oven-safe and with a lid. Cast iron or enamelled cast iron holds heat well.
For browning and turning the chicken pieces.

glossaryTerms.definition: The browned, sticky residue on the bottom of the pan after searing. Deglazing lifts it into the sauce, adding depth and colour.
A moist-heat cooking method: food is browned in fat, then cooked slowly in a covered pan with a small amount of liquid. The liquid and steam tenderise tough cuts and bone-in pieces over time.

ingredients.notes: Or a mix of thighs and drumsticks

steps: Heat the oven to 170 °C fan (190 °C / gas 5).

Season the chicken pieces generously with salt and pepper. Heat the olive oil in a large, wide casserole over a high heat. Brown the chicken skin-side down for 4–5 minutes until deeply golden, then turn and brown the other side for 2–3 minutes. Do this in two batches if necessary. Remove the chicken to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. In the fat remaining in the pan, cook the onion for 5 minutes until softened. Add the garlic for 1 minute.
Pour in the white wine and stir to lift the fond from the base. Let it bubble for 2 minutes until reduced by half.

Add the crushed tomatoes, chicken stock, rosemary, and thyme. Stir and bring to a simmer. Return the chicken pieces to the pan, skin-side up, pushing them down so they are mostly submerged. Add the olives and capers.
Transfer to the oven, uncovered, and braise for 45–50 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce has reduced and thickened. The skin should be lightly coloured on top.
Taste the sauce and adjust seasoning. Remove the rosemary and thyme sprigs. Serve from the pan with polenta, bread, or pasta.

content.text: The quality of the browning determines the quality of the dish. The chicken skin should be deeply, confidently golden before any liquid goes in — pale or anaemic browning produces a wan sauce with no depth. Dry the chicken with kitchen paper before seasoning to help the skin colour rather than steam.

Heat the oven to 170 °C fan. Season {{chicken-thigh-bone-in-skin-on}} of chicken with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide casserole over a high heat. Brown the chicken skin-side down for 4–5 minutes until deeply golden, then turn for 2–3 minutes more. Brown in batches — do not crowd the pan. Remove to a plate.
Reduce to medium heat. Cook the onion in the fat remaining in the pan for 5 minutes until soft. Add the garlic for 1 minute. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and stir to lift the

from the base. Let it reduce by half, about 2 minutes. Add the crushed tomatoes, {{chicken-stock}} of chicken stock, rosemary, and thyme. Bring to a simmer, then return the chicken skin-side up, tucking it into the sauce. Add the olives and capers.
Transfer to the oven uncovered and
for 45–50 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce has thickened. Remove the herb sprigs, taste and adjust seasoning, and serve from the pan.

This dish is one of those that genuinely improves overnight. The sauce deepens in flavour, the chicken softens further into the braising liquid, and the whole thing is easier to portion cold. Refrigerate overnight and reheat gently, covered, on the hob over a low heat with a splash of water to loosen.
